text: The surgeon develops the dissection space cephalad, extending just beyond the midline, and performs a similar dissection in the caudal direction around the hernia site, revealing the characteristic 'volcano sign' in the tissue.
The surgeon reduces the hernia sac, carefully dissecting it away from the pseudosac. The procedure progresses to developing the contralateral preperitoneal dissection.

The surgeon carefully dissects the transversalis fascia, identifying two leaflets to efficiently develop the space. Electrocautery is used to buzz and burn the tissue being pushed away, minimizing the risk of creating peritoneal holes.
The surgeon begins closing the hernia defect using a permanent V-lock suture, starting a few centimeters caudal to the defect and progressing cephalad.
The surgeon assesses the hernia defect, noting it is approximately 2.5 centimeters wide and 5 centimeters long.
The surgeon re-approximates the tissue edges and runs the suture back, carefully placing stitches between previous bites to avoid overplicating the tissue.
The peritoneal flap is visible in the foreground, positioned to accommodate a 15 by 15 mesh, providing broad coverage over the circumferentially closed hernia defect.
The surgeon assesses the positioning of the mesh over the hernia defect in the anterior abdominal wall and decides on two points of fixation due to the mesh sitting nicely.

The surgeon demonstrates the potential space created by the mesh placement, showing how desufflation of the abdomen results in the mesh closely adhering to the tissue. The procedure concludes with the closure of the peritoneal flap.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 V-lock suture on a V-20 needle, running the absorbable suture from left to right across the screen.
The surgeon uses a six-inch V-lock suture to close the peritoneal flap, ensuring the flap is securely closed. A small hole is visible around the previously repaired hernia defect.
The surgeon removes the polysorb absorbable braided suture used for mesh fixation, completing the procedure.
